subject: social-studies
title: Grade 8 Social Studies — U.S. History (Through 1877)
description: Texas Grade 8 SS follows TEKS §113.20 — U.S. History from colonization through Reconstruction. THIS IS THE STAAR-TESTED GRADE for Social Studies. Six core strands: Colonial America, Revolution & Constitution, Early Republic, Westward Expansion, Civil War, Reconstruction. Authored 2026-05-17.

lessons.title: Founding & Growth of the 13 Colonies
lessons.objective: Identify the founding, regions, and economies of the original 13 English colonies.

questions.prompt: Jamestown, founded in 1607, was the first PERMANENT English settlement in North America. It was located in what is now:
questions.answer: Virginia
questions.explanation: Jamestown was founded in 1607 in present-day Virginia as a business venture by the Virginia Company. It survived disease and starvation in its first years; the Plymouth Colony (Massachusetts) came later in 1620.

questions.prompt: The Mayflower Compact (1620) is considered an important early example of:
choices: Self-government — colonists agreeing to make and obey their own laws
questions.answer: Self-government — colonists agreeing to make and obey their own laws
questions.explanation: The Pilgrims signed the Mayflower Compact before landing at Plymouth. It established that they would form a government and obey "just and equal laws" agreed upon by the group — an early American example of self-government based on consent.

questions.prompt: The three colonial regions (New England, Middle, and Southern) had different economies because:
choices: England assigned each region a different job
Their geography and climate suited different industries — fishing/trade in New England, mixed farming in the Middle, plantation farming in the South
Each had different laws against business
questions.answer: Their geography and climate suited different industries — fishing/trade in New England, mixed farming in the Middle, plantation farming in the South
questions.explanation: Geography determined economy: rocky New England soil with great harbors led to fishing, shipbuilding, and trade. The Middle Colonies' fertile soil supported diverse farming and trade through cities like Philadelphia. Warm climates and rich soil in the South supported plantation cash crops (tobacco, rice, indigo, later cotton) — and the brutal expansion of slavery.

questions.prompt: The triangular trade involved exchanging which three things between which three regions?
choices: Cars between USA, Mexico, and Canada
Manufactured goods to Africa, enslaved Africans to the Americas, and raw materials (like sugar) back to Europe
Spices between China, India, and Japan
Whales between Greenland, Iceland, and Norway
questions.answer: Manufactured goods to Africa, enslaved Africans to the Americas, and raw materials (like sugar) back to Europe
questions.explanation: The triangular trade was a brutal Atlantic trade system: European manufactured goods (cloth, guns, rum) were traded for kidnapped Africans, who were forced across the Atlantic on the Middle Passage to work American plantations. Raw materials (sugar, tobacco, cotton) from those plantations went back to Europe.

questions.prompt: The French and Indian War (1754-1763) is important to American history because:
choices: It left Britain in debt and led to new taxes on the colonies — major causes of the Revolution
It united France and England forever
It had nothing to do with the colonies
questions.answer: It left Britain in debt and led to new taxes on the colonies — major causes of the Revolution
questions.explanation: Britain won the war (the colonies fought alongside Britain against France and France's Native American allies). But Britain emerged deeply in debt. To pay for the war and to defend the new territories, Britain imposed new taxes on the colonies — the Stamp Act, Sugar Act, Townshend Acts — sparking the resistance that became the Revolution.

lessons.title: From Revolution to the Constitution
lessons.objective: Analyze the causes of the Revolution and the structure of the Constitution.

questions.prompt: The slogan "No taxation without representation" expressed which colonial complaint?
choices: That colonists paid no taxes at all
That Britain taxed the colonies even though colonists had no elected members in the British Parliament
That taxes should be paid only by women
That all taxes should go to the colonies
questions.answer: That Britain taxed the colonies even though colonists had no elected members in the British Parliament
questions.explanation: Colonists objected that Parliament was passing taxes on them without their consent — they had no representatives in Parliament who could vote on those laws. This violated what colonists saw as their rights as English subjects.

questions.prompt: The Declaration of Independence (1776), written mainly by Thomas Jefferson, stated that all people are endowed with certain unalienable rights including:
choices: Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness
questions.answer: Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness
questions.explanation: The Declaration's most famous line says "all men are created equal" with unalienable rights to "Life, Liberty, and the pursuit of Happiness." These ideas — drawn from philosophers like John Locke — became foundational to American government, even though they applied unevenly to women, enslaved people, and Native Americans at the time.

questions.prompt: The Articles of Confederation, the FIRST government of the United States (1781-1789), failed because:
choices: It made the central government too weak — it couldn't tax, raise an army, or regulate trade between states
questions.answer: It made the central government too weak — it couldn't tax, raise an army, or regulate trade between states
questions.explanation: The Articles created a national government with almost no real powers — fearing tyranny, the founders went too far the other way. Without taxing power, an army, or trade regulation, the new nation couldn't function. That's why the Constitution (1787) created a stronger federal government.

questions.prompt: The U.S. Constitution divides the federal government into three branches. Which branch makes the laws?
questions.answer: Legislative (Congress)
questions.explanation: Congress (the Legislative branch) makes laws. The President (Executive) carries them out. The Supreme Court (Judicial) interprets them. The three branches check and balance each other so no single branch can take over.

questions.prompt: The Bill of Rights — the first 10 amendments to the Constitution — was added in 1791 mainly to:
choices: Give specific protections to individuals against government — like freedom of speech, religion, and the press
Set the value of the dollar
questions.answer: Give specific protections to individuals against government — like freedom of speech, religion, and the press
questions.explanation: Anti-Federalists worried the new Constitution gave too much power to the federal government. The Bill of Rights was added to guarantee specific individual rights: speech, religion, press, assembly, jury trial, no cruel punishments, and reserved powers to the states and the people.

lessons.title: Washington, Jefferson & the New Nation
lessons.objective: Identify key developments and decisions of the first presidents.

questions.prompt: George Washington, the first U.S. president, set what important precedent (tradition) about presidential terms?
choices: He served two terms and refused a third — establishing a tradition broken only once in U.S. history
He served until he died in office
questions.answer: He served two terms and refused a third — establishing a tradition broken only once in U.S. history
questions.explanation: Washington could likely have been president for life. Instead, he stepped down after two terms in 1797, setting a tradition that lasted 144 years — until Franklin D. Roosevelt won a third (and fourth) term during WWII. The 22nd Amendment (1951) made two terms the official limit.

questions.prompt: The Louisiana Purchase (1803), made by President Thomas Jefferson, did what?
choices: Doubled the size of the United States by buying a huge territory from France
questions.answer: Doubled the size of the United States by buying a huge territory from France
questions.explanation: Jefferson bought 828,000 square miles from France for $15 million — about 3 cents per acre. The Louisiana Territory included all or part of 15 modern states from Louisiana to Montana. It doubled the country's size and opened the West to American expansion.

questions.prompt: Marbury v. Madison (1803) was the Supreme Court case that established:
choices: The President's power to declare war
Judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution
That federal courts have no power
questions.answer: Judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution
questions.explanation: Chief Justice John Marshall ruled in Marbury v. Madison that the Supreme Court can declare a law unconstitutional. This power — judicial review — wasn't in the Constitution's text but has been one of the Court's most important powers ever since.

questions.prompt: The Monroe Doctrine (1823) warned European countries against:
choices: Establishing NEW colonies in the Western Hemisphere or interfering with independent nations there
questions.answer: Establishing NEW colonies in the Western Hemisphere or interfering with independent nations there
questions.explanation: President James Monroe declared that European powers should leave the Americas alone — no new colonies, no interference with existing independent nations. The U.S. promised in return to stay out of European affairs. The Monroe Doctrine became a foundation of U.S. foreign policy.

questions.prompt: The Industrial Revolution began transforming the U.S. economy in the early 1800s. One key American invention was:
choices: Eli Whitney's cotton gin (1793), which made cotton processing much faster — and tragically expanded slavery
questions.answer: Eli Whitney's cotton gin (1793), which made cotton processing much faster — and tragically expanded slavery
questions.explanation: Whitney's cotton gin removed seeds from cotton 50 times faster than by hand. This made cotton hugely profitable in the South — and led plantation owners to demand MORE enslaved labor, not less. Slavery was probably declining before the cotton gin; the gin reversed that and entrenched it for another 70 years.

lessons.title: Manifest Destiny, Mexican-American War & Sectionalism
lessons.objective: Analyze westward expansion and the growing tensions between North and South.

questions.prompt: "Manifest Destiny" was the 19th-century belief that:
choices: Americans were destined by God to expand across the entire North American continent
America should never expand beyond the original 13 colonies
The President should rule for life
questions.answer: Americans were destined by God to expand across the entire North American continent
questions.explanation: Coined in 1845, "Manifest Destiny" was the belief that the United States was meant to stretch from Atlantic to Pacific. The idea inspired westward migration, the Mexican-American War, and policies that displaced Native Americans — including the Trail of Tears in the 1830s.

questions.prompt: The Mexican-American War (1846-1848) ended with the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. The treaty gave the United States control of:
choices: California, Arizona, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Colorado/Wyoming — about 525,000 square miles
questions.answer: California, Arizona, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Colorado/Wyoming — about 525,000 square miles
questions.explanation: The Mexican Cession gave the U.S. roughly half of Mexico's territory in exchange for $15 million. This vast new land (modern California, Arizona, New Mexico, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Colorado, Wyoming, and Kansas) intensified the slavery debate — which territories would allow slavery?

questions.prompt: The Missouri Compromise (1820) tried to settle the slavery question by:
choices: Admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, and banning slavery in northern Louisiana Territory
Allowing Missouri to be split in half
Sending all enslaved people back to Africa
questions.answer: Admitting Missouri as a slave state and Maine as a free state, and banning slavery in northern Louisiana Territory
questions.explanation: The Missouri Compromise balanced one new slave state with one new free state, and drew a line (36°30' latitude) — slavery would be allowed south of the line but banned north of it. It held for over 30 years until the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) overturned it.

questions.prompt: The Trail of Tears (1838-1839) refers to:
choices: The forced removal of the Cherokee and other Southeast tribes to land west of the Mississippi, during which thousands died
questions.answer: The forced removal of the Cherokee and other Southeast tribes to land west of the Mississippi, during which thousands died
questions.explanation: President Andrew Jackson's Indian Removal Act forced about 60,000 Native Americans — including the Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, and Seminole — from their homes in the Southeast to "Indian Territory" (modern Oklahoma). Roughly 4,000 Cherokee died from disease, exposure, and starvation on the journey.

questions.prompt: The Compromise of 1850 included which controversial law that required Northerners to help capture escaped enslaved people?
questions.answer: The Fugitive Slave Act
questions.explanation: The Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 required Northerners (even in free states) to help return escaped enslaved people to their enslavers in the South — under threat of fines or jail. It outraged abolitionists and inspired Harriet Beecher Stowe to write "Uncle Tom's Cabin" (1852), which influenced public opinion against slavery.

lessons.title: Civil War: Causes, Course & Emancipation
lessons.objective: Analyze the causes and outcomes of the Civil War.

questions.prompt: The MOST fundamental cause of the Civil War was:
choices: Slavery — its expansion into new territories, its morality, and its role in the economy
questions.answer: Slavery — its expansion into new territories, its morality, and its role in the economy
questions.explanation: While related issues — states' rights, economic differences, political power — all mattered, slavery was the bedrock cause. Southern states' own secession documents explicitly cited slavery. Lincoln's election in 1860, won without any Southern electoral votes, signaled that the federal government would limit slavery's expansion — and the South seceded rather than accept that.

questions.prompt: The Civil War began on April 12, 1861, when Confederate forces attacked which federal fort?
questions.answer: Fort Sumter
questions.explanation: Confederate forces fired on Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor, South Carolina. The federal garrison surrendered after 34 hours of bombardment. President Lincoln then called for 75,000 volunteers to put down the rebellion — and the Civil War was on.

questions.prompt: The Emancipation Proclamation (January 1, 1863):
choices: Freed every enslaved person in the United States immediately
Declared enslaved people in Confederate-controlled areas free — turning the war into a war for freedom
questions.answer: Declared enslaved people in Confederate-controlled areas free — turning the war into a war for freedom
questions.explanation: Lincoln's proclamation freed enslaved people in CONFEDERATE-held areas (not in Union slave states like Maryland and Missouri). Its actual immediate effect was limited, but it transformed the war's purpose to include ending slavery, allowed Black men to fight for the Union army, and discouraged European countries from supporting the Confederacy.

questions.prompt: The Battle of Gettysburg (July 1863) is considered a turning point of the war because:
choices: It was the first battle of the war
It was the deadliest battle and ended the Confederate invasion of the North — the South was now on the defensive
Lincoln gave the Declaration of Independence there
questions.answer: It was the deadliest battle and ended the Confederate invasion of the North — the South was now on the defensive
questions.explanation: Over three days at Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, about 51,000 soldiers were killed, wounded, or captured — the war's bloodiest battle. Lee's defeat ended his second invasion of the North. From that point on, the Confederacy fought a defensive war they were slowly losing. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address — given at the dedication of the cemetery there — became one of the most famous American speeches.

questions.prompt: The Civil War ended on April 9, 1865, when General Robert E. Lee surrendered to General Ulysses S. Grant at:
questions.answer: Appomattox Court House
questions.explanation: Lee surrendered to Grant at the Appomattox Court House in Virginia. Grant offered generous terms — Confederate soldiers could go home rather than become prisoners. Other Confederate forces surrendered over the next few weeks. The war was over, but Lincoln was assassinated just five days later, leaving the work of Reconstruction to others.

lessons.title: 13th, 14th, 15th Amendments & Reconstruction
lessons.objective: Analyze the achievements and failures of Reconstruction.

questions.prompt: The 13th Amendment (1865) did what?
choices: Gave women the right to vote
Abolished slavery throughout the United States
Gave the federal government the power to tax incomes
questions.answer: Abolished slavery throughout the United States
questions.explanation: The 13th Amendment, ratified in December 1865 after the war, abolished slavery throughout the United States. The Emancipation Proclamation had only freed enslaved people in Confederate areas; the 13th Amendment finally ended it everywhere, permanently, as the supreme law of the land.

questions.prompt: The 14th Amendment (1868) is one of the most important amendments because it:
choices: Established Citizen-by-birth and equal protection under the law for all citizens
questions.answer: Established Citizen-by-birth and equal protection under the law for all citizens
questions.explanation: The 14th Amendment made anyone born in the United States a citizen (overturning the Dred Scott decision that had denied citizenship to Black Americans) and required states to provide "equal protection of the laws." These guarantees became the basis for many later civil rights rulings, including Brown v. Board of Education.

questions.prompt: The 15th Amendment (1870) granted what right to whom?
choices: Black men the right to vote — extending voting regardless of race or previous enslavement
All Americans the right to a free education
Everyone the right to own property
questions.answer: Black men the right to vote — extending voting regardless of race or previous enslavement
questions.explanation: The 15th Amendment said voting rights couldn't be denied based on "race, color, or previous condition of servitude." It enfranchised Black men. (Women — Black or white — would have to wait another 50 years for the 19th Amendment in 1920.) Many Black men served in state legislatures and Congress during Reconstruction.

questions.prompt: After Reconstruction ended in 1877, many Southern states passed laws to:
choices: Increase Black voting and economic opportunity
Roll back the rights Black Americans had gained — including through poll taxes, literacy tests, and Jim Crow segregation laws
Give every freedperson 100 acres of land
questions.answer: Roll back the rights Black Americans had gained — including through poll taxes, literacy tests, and Jim Crow segregation laws
questions.explanation: The compromise that ended Reconstruction in 1877 removed federal troops from the South. Southern states then passed "Jim Crow" laws that segregated public life, and used poll taxes, literacy tests, and other tactics to prevent Black Americans from voting. These restrictions weren't fully overturned until the Civil Rights Act (1964) and Voting Rights Act (1965).

questions.prompt: Why is Reconstruction often called "unfinished"?
choices: The federal government never ended it officially
Many of its promises — full citizenship, equal protection, voting rights for Black Americans — were undermined or ignored for nearly a century afterward
questions.answer: Many of its promises — full citizenship, equal protection, voting rights for Black Americans — were undermined or ignored for nearly a century afterward
questions.explanation: Reconstruction's constitutional amendments were revolutionary, but the country didn't actually deliver on them for almost 100 years. The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 1960s is often called the "Second Reconstruction" because it FINALLY enforced rights the 14th and 15th Amendments had promised in 1868 and 1870.
